summary:
The Sredny Stog culture occupied the Pontic steppe — the grasslands of present-day Ukraine — between approximately 4500 and 3500 BCE, representing one of the most important pre-Yamnaya cultures of the [[event:1538|Pontic-Caspian steppe]] zone. It is particularly significant for providing some of the earliest evidence of horse domestication and possibly riding in the western steppe, and for its cultural connections to both the agricultural societies of the Balkans and the pastoralist cultures of the eastern steppe.

Sredny Stog communities were semi-mobile pastoralists who herded cattle, sheep, and horses, moving seasonally across the steppe while maintaining some degree of territorial identity. The culture is associated with the early development of kurgan burial mounds — low earthen tumuli covering pit graves — that would become the characteristic mortuary monument of steppe cultures for the following two millennia. Evidence from Sredny Stog sites has been cited in support of early horse domestication: horse bones constitute a significant proportion of faunal assemblages, and some researchers interpret this as evidence of herding rather than merely hunting.

The Sredny Stog culture interacted with the [[event:38|Cucuteni-Trypillia culture]] to its west and contributed to the broader cultural dynamics of the steppe that eventually produced the Yamnaya culture, widely associated with the spread of [[event:1544|Proto-Indo-European languages]] across Eurasia.
